item.title: Hmm.
item.content:encoded:
I'm really not sure what to think about this. It really feels like something's missing, and reading the full article just made me confused — I wondered if that was really it, and whether or not I missed a larger narrative going on. I suppose this article could be described as too Series 1-esque for my tastes.

Not to mention that the implication that this anomaly caused the devastating 2011 earthquake and tsunami was really disappointing. It hasn't even been 10 years, and saying that some anomaly caused a very real disaster is something that has generally been frowned upon — not to mention cheap and disrespectful to victims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, though I do need to point out that the Earthquake and subsequent disaster at the Fukushima Daiichi reactor was in 2011, not 2015.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Always love me some IJAMEA goodness

I like the Onryo protocol, especially because I can imagine a family essentially being raised just to commit this act.

Also shouldn't this thing be keter? Since you have to perform a very specific ritual to keep it contained, and since that ritual is essentially out of Foundation hands. Which they'd probably see as much worse.

Either way that's a pretty minor nitpick. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a very interesting read. I found the containment procedures to be a bit extreme, although I am in the same boat as silentquirrel, they sold this article for me. A bit tense and unsettling to go through, and the completion of the ritual is just brutal. Well done <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy the staging for the three acts of the containment protocol. The requirement for the three participants to be willing adds a certain element of dread to the proceedings and the fact that only one recorded protocol wasn't carried out gives me the impression that the sense of duty to the country carried the participants in other attempts through to completion.
Tying in actual disasters for timing purposes is also a nice touch, +1 from me.

(I noticed an extra 't' in 'notified' in the third paragraph.
The second bullet point in the description has 'proceeding the event' and I'm not sure if it should be 'preceding the event' or not? 'Proceeding' is used again at the end of the protocol, outside of the inset. If that is deliberate I apologize, it just seems out of place to me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the last line of the table of what events happend you wrote that

A Fukushū Event occurred at 4:20am on 20/12/1946
which caused the Fukusima reactor breakdown.

I think it is a typo but please correct me if isn't

item.title: Sticks and Stones, Blood for Old Bones, a Curse Forever Thirsty.
item.content:encoded:
So I drew a lot of inspiration from the real world legends of Emperor Sutoku, which has always been one of my favorite Japanese folklore stories. I wanted to write something where the Foundation faces the repercussions of their actions, to show that maybe not all anomalies are better off in Foundation hands. I hope y'all enjoy it.

Picture Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sotoku_invoking_a_thunder_storm.jpg

~~~More Stuff By Me~~~
